candidates.diagnosis: Bilateral pulmonary embolism with saddle embolus
supporting_evidence: Convergent VTE risk factors with multiplicative risk >30× baseline: drospirenone OCP (OR 6.3) + prolonged immobilization (12h car drive, OR 2.3) + family DVT history (inherited thrombophilia) + prior missed DVT (calf pain 3 weeks ago without Doppler)
Modified Wells score calculation: clinical signs consistent with DVT (prior calf pain) +3, PE most likely diagnosis +3, tachycardia >100 +1.5, immobilization +1.5 = 9+ points — PE LIKELY category
Bilateral PE should be suspected because the thrombus source (right leg DVT) has had 3 weeks to propagate, fragment, and embolize to both pulmonary arteries
Saddle embolus (straddling the main pulmonary artery bifurcation) is possible given the prolonged embolization timeline and large thrombus burden
SpO2 95% in a 28-year-old represents significant V/Q mismatch — this is NOT normal

candidates.diagnosis: Deep vein thrombosis — right lower extremity, propagated from prior missed diagnosis
supporting_evidence: Right calf pain 3 weeks ago diagnosed as 'muscle strain' at prior ED visit — this was the index DVT event
NO Doppler ultrasound was performed — the DVT was missed due to anchoring on 'muscle strain'
Over 3 weeks without anticoagulation, the thrombus has propagated from calf veins to proximal veins (popliteal → femoral → iliac)
Fragments from this propagated DVT are the source of the current pulmonary emboli
Lower extremity Doppler MUST be performed to assess residual thrombus burden

candidates.diagnosis: Right ventricular strain from pulmonary embolism
supporting_evidence: Tachycardia (HR 102) may represent early RV decompensation from acute pressure overload
RV strain can progress rapidly to RV failure and obstructive shock
Bedside echocardiography should assess RV dilation, McConnell sign, tricuspid regurgitation
Troponin and BNP/NT-proBNP elevation would confirm myocardial injury from RV strain
If RV failure is present, thrombolysis or catheter-directed therapy must be considered

candidates.diagnosis: Pulmonary infarction with possible abscess formation
supporting_evidence: Low-grade fever 37.2°C + pleuritic pain = classic pulmonary infarction
Infarction occurs in 10-30% of PE cases, more common with peripheral emboli
If infarction zone becomes infected, pulmonary abscess can develop — rare but serious
Chest X-ray may show Hampton's hump (wedge-shaped opacity) or Westermark sign (oligemia)
Follow-up imaging essential to monitor for cavitation

candidates.diagnosis: Inherited thrombophilia unmasked by OCP use
supporting_evidence: Family history of DVT in paternal aunt at age 45 — strongly suggests autosomal dominant thrombophilia
Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited thrombophilia in Swedish population (5% carrier rate — highest in Northern Europe)
Prothrombin gene mutation G20210A (2-3% prevalence) is the second most common
Drospirenone OCP + Factor V Leiden heterozygosity = synergistic VTE risk (OR 28.5 in published studies)
The OCP unmasked the thrombophilia — this patient may never have developed VTE without the hormonal trigger
Thrombophilia panel should be sent AFTER acute treatment (not during acute phase — false results); if positive, lifelong anticoagulation and genetic counselling for family members

reasoning: DEVIL'S ADVOCATE ASSESSMENT: I am deliberately weighting ALL diagnoses toward VTE-spectrum pathology because this case has EVERY red flag for a missed PE. The cognitive trap here is classic anchoring bias: a young, healthy-appearing woman with reproducible chest wall tenderness after physical exertion — the clinician's brain screams 'musculoskeletal' and stops thinking. But the EHR tells a completely different story. The drospirenone OCP (highest VTE risk class), 12-hour car drive (prolonged immobilization), family DVT history (inherited thrombophilia), and most damningly the PRIOR ED VISIT for right calf pain that was dismissed as muscle strain WITHOUT DOPPLER IMAGING — this is a MISSED DVT that has progressed to PE over 3 weeks. The medical literature is clear: chest wall tenderness has NO discriminative value for excluding PE (present in 19.9% of PE patients vs 23.8% of non-PE, p=0.25). The Modified Wells score exceeds 9 points. D-dimer should NOT be used as a screening tool when clinical probability is this high — go directly to CTPA. Every minute of delay increases the risk of cardiovascular collapse from RV failure. This patient needs STAT imaging, immediate anticoagulation, and ICU standby.
